flashcards.front: Who invented the Turing Machine and in what year?
flashcards.back: Alan Turing invented the Turing Machine in 1936 in his paper 'On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem'.

flashcards.front: What are the seven main components of a Turing Machine?
flashcards.back: 1) Tape (infinite memory), 2) Read-Write Head, 3) State Register, 4) Transition Function, 5) Start State, 6) Accept State(s), 7) Reject State(s).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a Deterministic Turing Machine (DTM) and Non-Deterministic Turing Machine (NTM)?
flashcards.back: DTM has exactly one transition for each state-symbol pair, while NTM can have multiple possible transitions for the same state-symbol pair.

flashcards.front: What does it mean for a language to be Turing-recognizable (recursively enumerable)?
flashcards.back: A language is Turing-recognizable if there exists a Turing Machine that halts and accepts all strings in the language, and may either reject or loop forever on strings not in the language.
It states that any computable function can be computed by a Turing Machine. It effectively defines the limits of what algorithms can accomplish.

flashcards.front: What is a Universal Turing Machine (UTM)?
flashcards.back: A UTM is a Turing Machine that can simulate any other Turing Machine by taking as input the encoding of the other TM and the input to that TM.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Turing-decidable and Turing-recognizable languages?
flashcards.back: Turing-decidable languages: TM always halts (accepts or rejects). Turing-recognizable languages: TM may loop forever on non-members but halts on members.

flashcards.front: What are the three types of moves a Turing Machine head can make?
flashcards.back: 1) Move Left (L), 2) Move Right (R), 3) Stay in place (S) - though standard TM only uses L and R.
The problem of determining, given any program and input, whether the program will halt (finish execution) or run forever. It is undecidable.

flashcards.front: What is a multi-tape Turing Machine?
flashcards.back: A TM with multiple tapes, each having its own read-write head. It can simulate any single-tape TM and is useful for proving time complexity relationships.

flashcards.front: What is the configuration of a Turing Machine?
flashcards.back: A configuration is represented as (current state, current tape content, head position). It completely describes the current state of the computation.

flashcards.front: What is the language of a Turing Machine?
flashcards.back: The set of all strings (inputs) for which the Turing Machine enters an accept state. It is called the language recognized by that TM.

flashcards.front: What is a linear bounded automaton (LBA)?
flashcards.back: A restricted Turing Machine where the tape is bounded by the length of the input. It can recognize context-sensitive languages but not all recursively enumerable languages.

flashcards.front: What does it mean for a function to be computable?
flashcards.back: A function is computable if there exists a Turing Machine that, given any input, produces the correct output and halts.

flashcards.front: What is the acceptance criterion for a Turing Machine?
flashcards.back: The TM accepts an input string if it enters an accept state at any point during computation. It rejects if it enters a reject state.
